title: Mille-feuille
subtitle: Flaky rough puff layered with vanilla pastry cream and iced
excerpt: Three layers of shatteringly crisp rough puff pastry, two layers of thick vanilla pastry cream, and a white icing with a dark chocolate feather pattern across the top. One of the classic French pastries, made accessible with rough puff in place of full puff.
sourceNotes: Mille-feuille is documented in French cuisine from the seventeenth century and codified in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), as a standard French pastry of puff pastry sheets layered with pastry cream and iced. The name meaning 'a thousand leaves' refers to the many layers of the pastry. The feather-icing decoration across the top is a standard technique documented in mid-twentieth-century French patisserie manuals. The recipe is fully public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rough puff sheets and pastry cream can be made a day ahead and refrigerated separately. Assemble within two hours of serving; the pastry softens quickly once in contact with the cream.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 220°C. Press the pastry flat mid-bake with a second tray to ensure even layers.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, cooked custard made from milk, egg yolks, sugar, and flour or cornflour. Used as a filling for tarts, pastries, and choux. Sets firmer than pouring custard when chilled.
A shortcut puff pastry made by incorporating cold butter in larger pieces than shortcrust, then folding the dough several times. It produces flaky layers without the precision of full puff.

content.text: Mille-feuille is a pastry of contrasts: the shattering crispness of the pastry layers against the soft, cold

pastry used here gives excellent results in a fraction of the time of full puff; the layers are not as numerous or as even, but they blister and shatter in the same way when baked correctly. The key is pressing the layers flat with a second tray halfway through baking — this prevents the pastry from puffing too dramatically and gives clean, stackable sheets.

Mille-feuille is assembled close to serving. The pastry softens within a couple of hours of contact with the cream and loses its defining crispness. Bake the pastry and make the cream ahead; assemble shortly before eating.

attrs.prepNote: for the rough puff; plus 20 g for the pastry cream
very cold, for the rough puff; plus 30 g for the pastry cream
split and scraped, for the pastry cream

content.text: Make the rough puff and bake the sheets

Mix the pastry-quantity {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add the pastry-quantity very cold {{unsalted-butter}} in rough 1 cm pieces and toss briefly in the flour. Add 80–90 ml of ice-cold water and mix to a rough dough with visible butter pieces. Roll to a rectangle, fold in thirds, rotate, and roll again; repeat twice. Wrap and refrigerate for 60 minutes. Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Roll the chilled pastry to a rectangle about 3 mm thick and cut into three equal strips of identical size. Place on lined baking trays. Bake for 12 minutes, then lay a sheet of baking paper over the pastry and press down firmly with a second tray to flatten. Return to the oven for 10–12 minutes more until deeply golden and crisp throughout. Cool completely on a rack.

Bring {{whole-milk}} and the seeds and pod from {{vanilla-pod}} to a gentle simmer. Whisk {{egg-yolks}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and the pastry cream-quantity {{plain-flour}} together in a bowl until smooth. Pour the hot milk over the egg mixture, whisking constantly, then return to the pan. Cook over medium heat, stirring continuously, until the cream thickens and boils for 1–2 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in the pastry cream-quantity {{unsalted-butter}}. Remove the vanilla pod. Press cling film directly onto the surface and refrigerate until cold and firm, at least 90 minutes.

Beat the chilled pastry cream to loosen it. Spread or pipe half the cream evenly over one pastry sheet. Place the second sheet on top and spread the remaining cream over it. Place the third sheet on top, pressing gently. Mix {{icing-sugar}} with enough cold water (about 2–3 tablespoons) to make a thick, spreadable icing. Spread evenly over the top sheet. Pipe lines of melted {{dark-chocolate}} across the icing at 1 cm intervals. Drag a skewer or knife tip across the chocolate lines alternately in opposite directions to create the feather pattern. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to set, then trim the edges with a sharp serrated knife and slice.
A serrated bread knife cuts mille-feuille without compressing the layers. Saw gently rather than pressing straight down.
For a raspberry mille-feuille: fold 150 g of fresh raspberries into the pastry cream before assembling, and scatter a few on top of the cream layers.
The pastry cream can be made up to two days ahead. Whisk briskly before using if it has set very firm in the fridge.
